title: Cloud Under Fire
subtitle: Hyperscale Data Centers and the Rise of Cyber-Physical Warfare
title_normalized: cloud under fire: hyperscale data centers and the rise of cyber-physical warfare

alternate_identifiers.identifier: Wu (2026), Cloud Under Fire, EPINOVA Working Paper F–2026–05
alternate_identifiers.description: Short citation implied by the EPINOVA working paper format

description: This working paper examines the militarization of hyperscale cloud infrastructure and the rise of cyber-physical warfare through the concept of Digital Strategic Nodes. Using reported drone strikes affecting infrastructure near AWS cloud facilities in the United Arab Emirates and Bahrain during the 2026 U.S.–Iran conflict as a case study, it maps major cloud regions, Internet exchange points, submarine cable landing stations, cloud interconnection facilities, and edge-distribution nodes across the Middle East and analyzes how physical attacks on digital infrastructure may generate cyber-like disruption.
abstract: The rapid expansion of cloud computing has concentrated critical digital services within a relatively small number of hyperscale data centers operated by major technology firms. These facilities support financial systems, artificial intelligence platforms, logistics networks, and government operations, making them essential components of contemporary economic and technological systems. As a result, cloud infrastructure may increasingly represent a potential category of strategic targets in modern conflict. This article examines the emerging militarization of digital infrastructure through the case of reported drone strikes affecting infrastructure near Amazon Web Services (AWS) cloud facilities in the United Arab Emirates and Bahrain during the 2026 U.S.–Iran conflict. Building on the concept of Digital Strategic Nodes, the paper argues that hyperscale data centers represent highly concentrated points of systemic digital dependence and may therefore become attractive targets for adversaries seeking strategic disruption. Drawing on international relations scholarship on cyber conflict, critical infrastructure security, and cyber-physical systems, the article maps major digital infrastructure across the Middle East. The analysis identifies an expanded inventory of 28 digital strategic nodes, including hyperscale cloud regions, Internet exchange points, submarine cable landing stations, cloud interconnection facilities, and edge-distribution nodes located within the strike range of Iranian missile systems. Depending on whether secondary interconnection and edge facilities are included, the regional network comprises roughly 20–30 strategically significant nodes. The findings suggest that contemporary warfare is increasingly characterized by cyber-physical interaction, in which kinetic attacks on digital infrastructure may produce effects traditionally associated with cyber operations. This dynamic complicates existing theories of cyber deterrence and highlights the growing strategic importance of protecting large-scale digital infrastructure in future conflicts.

series_information.series_description: The WP-F series explores AI governance and strategic risk through comparative and historical diagnostics, examining how inherited strategic and regulatory logics break down when transferred into AI-mediated contexts.

methodology.framework_name: Digital Strategic Nodes (DSN) and Cyber-Physical Warfare Framework
methodology.case_study: Reported drone strikes affecting infrastructure near Amazon Web Services cloud facilities in the United Arab Emirates and Bahrain during the 2026 U.S.–Iran conflict
methodology.research_question: How does the physical targeting of cloud infrastructure reshape the cyber-physical dynamics of modern warfare?
methodology.method_type: Qualitative strategic analysis combined with empirical infrastructure mapping of selected Middle East digital infrastructure nodes.

core_concept.name: Digital Strategic Nodes
core_concept.definition: Infrastructure locations whose disruption may generate disproportionate systemic effects due to their central role within digital networks.

conceptual_model.formula: DSN Risk ∝ Concentration × Connectivity × Geographic Exposure

variables.name: Concentration
variables.description: Degree to which computing capacity and digital services are centralized within a limited number of facilities.

variables.name: Connectivity
variables.description: Extent to which a node functions as a junction within regional or global data networks, such as Internet exchange points or submarine cable landing stations.

variables.name: Geographic Exposure
variables.description: Physical accessibility of infrastructure within the strike range of conventional military capabilities, including missiles or drones.

regional_mapping.node_inventory_range: roughly 20–30 strategically significant nodes depending on inclusion of secondary interconnection and edge facilities

analytical_claims: Hyperscale cloud facilities combine high concentration and high connectivity, giving them potential strategic significance as Digital Strategic Nodes.
Kinetic attacks on digital infrastructure may generate cyber-like disruption without requiring digital intrusion.
Cyber deterrence and infrastructure security must expand beyond network defense to include physical protection, redundancy, and resilience of digital infrastructure nodes.
limitations: The case study relies on open-source reporting and publicly available information regarding reported strikes and service disruptions.
Range bands are intended as analytical visualization of layered reach rather than a precise probability model of strike likelihood.
Distance estimates are approximate great-circle estimates from western Iran and rounded to the nearest ten kilometers.
The paper analyzes strategic exposure and cyber-physical dynamics; it does not provide operational targeting guidance.

dimensions.name: Infrastructure Concentration
dimensions.description: Degree to which computing capacity and digital services are centralized within limited physical facilities.

dimensions.name: Network Connectivity
dimensions.description: Position of a facility as a junction for Internet routing, data exchange, submarine cable traffic, cloud interconnection, or edge distribution.

dimensions.name: Geographic Exposure
dimensions.description: Degree to which a digital infrastructure node lies within the reach of missiles, drones, or other conventional strike capabilities.

dimensions.name: Cyber-Physical Interaction
dimensions.description: Mechanism through which physical attacks on digital infrastructure produce digital service disruption, cyber-like outages, or network instability.

dimensions.name: Systemic Dependence
dimensions.description: Extent to which governments, financial systems, logistics networks, AI platforms, and private-sector services depend on the node.

dimensions.name: Cascade Potential
dimensions.description: Potential for localized disruption to propagate across interconnected cloud, network, economic, or governmental systems.

dimensions.name: Civilian-Military Ambiguity
dimensions.description: Degree to which cloud infrastructure supports both civilian services and strategically relevant governmental, economic, or technological functions.

key_findings: Cloud infrastructure may increasingly represent a potential category of strategic target in modern conflict because hyperscale data centers support finance, AI platforms, logistics networks, and government operations.
Hyperscale data centers operate as Digital Strategic Nodes because they concentrate systemic digital dependence within a limited number of highly connected facilities.
The mapped Middle East network includes an expanded inventory of 28 digital strategic nodes, with many falling within approximate 1,000 km, 1,500 km, or 2,000 km range bands from western Iran.
Kinetic attacks on physical cloud or network infrastructure may produce effects traditionally associated with cyber operations, including service disruptions, network outages, and economic losses.
Cyber-physical warfare challenges conventional distinctions between cyber operations and traditional military action by linking physical strikes to digital-system effects.
The centralization of cloud markets and digital services may create systemic vulnerabilities, because disruption of a small number of nodes could affect multiple sectors simultaneously.

Cyber deterrence strategies may need to expand beyond digital intrusion prevention to include physical protection of data centers, Internet exchange points, cable landing stations, and cloud interconnection facilities.
The militarization of hyperscale cloud infrastructure raises legal and normative questions about the protection of civilian digital systems during armed conflict.

figures.title: Conceptual model of DSN risk

figures.source: Author's conceptual model based on the Digital Strategic Nodes framework
figures.note: Analytical visualization of DSN risk; not a quantitative risk score.

figures.title: Digital Strategic Nodes Within Layered Iranian Missile Range Bands in the Middle East
figures.description: Map overlaying approximate 300 km, 700 km, 1,000 km, 1,500 km, and 2,000 km range bands from western Iran with selected hyperscale cloud infrastructure, Internet exchange points, submarine cable landing stations, cloud interconnection hubs, and edge-distribution nodes across the Middle East.
figures.source: Author's mapping based on public information on regional digital infrastructure and layered missile range bands
figures.note: Approximate analytical visualization of layered reach; not a precise probability model of strike likelihood.

tables.title: Digital Strategic Nodes in the Middle East
tables.description: Expanded inventory of 28 digital strategic nodes across the Middle East, including hyperscale cloud facilities, Internet exchanges, cable landing stations, cloud interconnection nodes, and edge/CDN nodes, with approximate distance from western Iran and corresponding missile range band.
tables.source: Author's compilation from public cloud, Internet exchange, cable landing, and regional infrastructure information
tables.note: Distances are approximate great-circle estimates measured from western Iran near Kermanshah and rounded to the nearest ten kilometers.

tables.title: Node Distribution by Range Band
tables.description: Summary distribution of mapped digital strategic nodes by missile range band: four nodes at or below 1,000 km, nineteen nodes in the 1,000–1,500 km band, and five nodes in the 1,500–2,000 km band.
tables.source: Author's calculation from the expanded inventory presented in Table 1
tables.note: Counts reflect approximate classification based on distance from western Iran using the Kermanshah reference point.

disclaimer: This metadata record is generated from the uploaded PDF and early ORCID-derived metadata. The working paper presents diagnostic and exploratory analysis intended to examine inherited strategic and governance logics under AI-mediated conditions. It does not constitute policy recommendations, predictive assessments, operational targeting guidance, or official positions of any institution.
